private_typical_coverage: Generally covered with autism/ID/DD diagnosis; ACMG first-tier test
coverage_notes: Fragile X testing is reimbursable by most payers (Medicare, Medi-Cal, most commercial) when billed with autism (F84.0), unexplained ID (F70-F79), or DD (F80-F89). 81243 is the PCR; 81244 is reflex methylation analysis if expansion detected. Standard of care per ACMG; denial is rare with documented developmental concern. Order through pediatrician or geneticist.

private_typical_coverage: Often covered for children under 18 with autism/DD/ID but prior auth required; trio sequencing (proband+parents, 81416 x2) frequently denied
coverage_notes: WES is the highest-yield diagnostic test (~25-30% post-CMA). Coverage improved markedly post-2020 - UHC, BCBS, Cigna, Aetna now treat as medically necessary when patient is <18 with one or more congenital anomalies plus developmental delay, ID, or ASD with no identified cause and prior testing (CMA, FXS) negative. Prior auth required. Trio (81415 proband + 81416 x2 parents) preferred for interpretation but capped at 2 units of 81416 by most payers. GeneDx, Invitae offer patient-responsibility cap programs ($100-$250 OOP).

private_typical_coverage: Not covered by any major commercial plan; specialty lab
coverage_notes: FRAT (folate receptor alpha autoantibody) test from Religious Sisters of Mercy / ReligenDx is cash-pay. $295 lab fee alone; $595 with required physician consult. 86376 is the generic autoimmune antibody CPT but no payer recognizes this specific assay. HSA/FSA eligible with LMN citing autism (F84.0) plus suspected cerebral folate deficiency (E53.8). Workaround: a clinician can bill the consult under E/M codes (99213/99214) covered separately. Insurance reimbursement is extremely rare even with appeal.

private_typical_coverage: Standard hospital lab order; covered with mitochondrial / encephalopathy / lactic acidosis diagnosis
coverage_notes: Lactate (83605) and pyruvate (84210) bill separately; the L:P ratio is calculated. Both covered when ICD-10 indicates suspected mitochondrial disorder (E88.40, E88.49), metabolic encephalopathy (G93.41), or lactic acidosis (E87.2). Pre-analytical handling is the practical problem - lactate requires immediate deproteinization. Send to hospital lab when possible. ICD-10 F84.0 alone may trigger denial; pair with G93.41 or E88.40.

private_typical_coverage: Mosaic Diagnostics is out-of-network with all commercial plans; cash-pay or partial superbill reimbursement
coverage_notes: The Organic Acids Test billed by Mosaic Diagnostics (formerly Great Plains) uses CPT 82542 (column chromatography, quantitative, each analyte) for each of 76 markers, but Mosaic is OON with all commercial plans. Mosaic provides a superbill for patient-submitted out-of-network claims; reimbursement is highly variable. Direct-pay $299-$399 depending on add-ons. HSA/FSA eligible without LMN per Mosaic billing guide (qualifies as diagnostic test). Genova Organix (TEST-0011) is a marginally lower-cost insurance-billable alternative ($179 copay through Genova-Connect).

private_typical_coverage: Aetna calls it experimental/investigational; ~50% of orders get partial reimbursement
coverage_notes: Moleculera Labs Cunningham Panel (5 markers: anti-D1, anti-D2, anti-lyso, anti-tubulin, CaMKII) bills under five separate 83520 immunoassay codes plus 86376. Aetna officially classifies as experimental/investigational. Roughly half of orders get partial reimbursement; complete coverage is rare. Cash-pay $995; insurance-attempt option requires $495 deposit. ICD-10 anchor F50.82 (ARFID) is one path; F95.x tic disorders, F42 OCD, or G93.41 encephalopathy stronger. Strongest claim: acute neuropsychiatric symptoms within 6 weeks of strep documented.

private_typical_coverage: FDA Breakthrough Device but not covered by any commercial plan as of 2026
coverage_notes: Clarifi ASD by Quadrant Biosciences uses PLA (Proprietary Laboratory Analyses) CPT code 0170U. CMS set the gapfill payment rate at $1,950 but assigned no NCD; no commercial plan covers as of 2026. Quadrant offers introductory $989 cash price. Test is positioned as 'aid in diagnosis' not standalone diagnostic; coverage will hinge on Medicaid pediatric coverage decisions. FDA Breakthrough Device designation does not equal coverage. HSA/FSA eligible as diagnostic test.

private_typical_coverage: Doctor's Data is OON with most commercial plans; insurance-billing option available with higher list price
coverage_notes: Doctor's Data Methylation Profile bills SAM and SAH under 82542 (chromatography) and homocysteine, cystathionine, methionine under 82136 (amino acids; each specimen). The lab offers two prices: 'prompt payment' (cash) $180-220, and 'insurance/Medicare' list at higher rate. Insurance reimbursement is unpredictable; many patients get $0-$50 back. The simpler Quest/LabCorp panel of homocysteine (83090) + MMA (83921) + plasma B12 (82607) + RBC folate (82747) captures most of the same clinical signal AT INSURANCE COVERAGE and runs $40-200 (TEST-0013). FM practitioners often run the comprehensive Doctor's Data only when Quest baseline shows borderline values.

private_typical_coverage: Not insurance-billable; cash-pay only
coverage_notes: TruDiagnostic TruAge with DunedinPACE is a laboratory-developed test (LDT) with no AMA CPT code. Not billable to any payer. Cash-pay $229 (TruAge Pace only) to $499 (TruAge Complete). HSA/FSA eligible per TruDiagnostic - they explicitly market HSA/FSA acceptance. Validated for ages 18-70 only; pediatric use is experimental and rarely informative for autism specifically. Use case: track maternal biological age + intervention response, not child's autism profile.

private_typical_coverage: Practitioner-only test; no insurance billing
coverage_notes: IntellxxDNA NeuroGenomic report is a clinician-only nutrigenomic panel. No CPT code; the company explicitly states reports are not covered by insurance. Cash-pay $500-$1500 depending on which panel (Mental Wellness vs full Neurodevelopmental). HSA/FSA eligible with LMN. Critical caveat: genetic information enters medical records and is reviewable by life/disability/long-term care insurance underwriters. Order only through a credentialed MAPS/IntellxxDNA-trained clinician. Lower-cost alternative: order 23andMe ($99-199) plus Genetic Genie ($10) or StrateGene ($75) for ~80% of the actionable methylation/detox SNP coverage.

private_typical_coverage: Standard clinical genetics workup; covered with FAO disorder / suspected mito
coverage_notes: Mayo's Acylcarnitine Profile (ACRN) bills under 82017. Insurance covers reliably when ordered with ICD-10 indicating suspected fatty acid oxidation defect (E71.30), carnitine deficiency (E71.41), or mitochondrial disorder (E88.40). Often part of standard newborn screen panel (already paid for). Autism dx alone (F84.0) may trigger denial; pair with E71.30 or E88.40 if elevated lactate or unusual carnitine pattern documented. ARUP, LabCorp, Quest all offer.

private_typical_coverage: Diagnostic Solutions Lab is OON with all commercial plans; some HSA/FSA reimbursement
coverage_notes: GI-MAP uses CPT 87507 (infectious agent NAAT, multiplex, GI pathogen, 12-25 targets). Code is legitimate and Medicare-recognized for the multiplex GI pathogen panel format, but Diagnostic Solutions is OON with all commercial plans and not contracted with Medicare. Patients receive superbill for self-submission. With strong medical necessity letter (chronic GI symptoms, failed empirical treatment, suspected dysbiosis), partial reimbursement possible. HSA/FSA eligible. Mainstream alternative: PCR Gastrointestinal Pathogen Panel through Quest/LabCorp ($150-300 covered) gives ~30% of the GI-MAP marker coverage at full coverage.

private_typical_coverage: Standard clinical workup when IEM suspected
coverage_notes: Plasma amino acids panel bills under 82139 (amino acids, multiple, quantitative). Quest test code 767. Covered with ICD-10 indicating amino acid metabolism disorder (E70-E72), inborn error of metabolism, or failure to thrive. Autism (F84.0) alone may not trigger coverage; pair with E72.9 (disorder of amino-acid metabolism, unspecified) or R62.51 (failure to thrive, child) for cleanest claim path. Strongest justification when prior unexplained ID, regression, or organic-acid abnormality on OAT documented.

private_typical_coverage: Not insurance billable; FSA/HSA eligible if health-only kit purchased
coverage_notes: 23andMe Health + Ancestry is a consumer genetic testing service. Not billable to insurance. Health-only kit (without ancestry component) is FSA/HSA-eligible per 23andMe's own ruling-supported guidance; up to $174 may be reimbursable. Ancestry-only kits do NOT qualify. Bankruptcy proceedings 2025-26 introduce some data-retention uncertainty - download raw .txt file within 30 days of receiving results. Same FSA/HSA framework applies to AncestryDNA Health (TEST-0002).

private_typical_coverage: Software-only product; cash-pay
coverage_notes: StrateGene by Dr Lynch (sold through Sequencing.com) is a software report run against uploaded raw SNP data. No CPT code; no insurance billing. Cash-pay $75-150. HSA/FSA eligible with LMN if part of treatment plan for diagnosed condition. Similar story for Genetic Genie methylation report ($0-15) and NutraHacker reports ($22-57) which are also TEST-0003 and TEST-0004 in the catalog.

private_typical_coverage: Standard preventive lab; ACA preventive-care mandate covers annually
coverage_notes: CBC (85025) + CMP (80053) + lipid panel (80061) are standard well-child labs. ACA preventive-care mandate requires one annual CMP-equivalent covered $0 copay for routine physical. Diagnostic context (ICD-10 F84.0 plus presenting symptoms) covers add-on testing same encounter. The 'always do this first' lab. CPT 80050 General Health Panel bundles CBC+CMP+TSH and if billed precludes itemized billing of 80053 same day.

private_typical_coverage: Covered with documented deficiency or specific risk-factor ICD-10; routine screening denied
coverage_notes: Vitamin D 25-OH (82306) is covered when billed with ICD-10 indicating documented deficiency (E55.9), at-risk condition (osteomalacia M83.x, malabsorption K90.x, chronic kidney disease, anticonvulsant therapy, dark skin in non-tropical latitude). Routine screening (Z13.220) is denied by Medicare and most commercial plans. For autism, the cleanest path is to test once under risk-screening (E55.9 + F84.0), document deficiency, then retest under E55.9. Pediatric Medicaid covers more permissively than Medicare. LetsGetChecked and Everlywell DTC versions $30-100 cash, FSA/HSA eligible.

private_typical_coverage: Cheap DTC microbiome composition test; cash-pay only
coverage_notes: Biomesight is direct-to-consumer 16S rRNA microbiome sequencing. No CPT code; no insurance billing path. Cash-pay $99-149. HSA/FSA eligibility ambiguous - sold as wellness rather than diagnostic; may qualify with LMN if ordered as part of diagnostic workup for documented GI symptoms (K59.x, K90.x). Compare to GI-MAP (TEST-0025) which is clinical-grade qPCR with pathogen-detection capability; Biomesight is composition-only.

private_typical_coverage: FDA-labeled for cerebral folate deficiency as of 2025; generic widely covered
coverage_notes: Leucovorin (folinic acid) generic 5/10/15/25mg tablets are widely covered on commercial formularies (typically Tier 1-2). FDA label expansion 2025 added cerebral folate deficiency as an approved indication, which triggers mandatory Medicaid/CHIP coverage nationally. For autism with FRAT-positive results, prescribe under E53.8 (other specified vitamin B deficiencies) - that ICD-10 maps to the new approved label. Off-label autism (F84.0 alone) may trigger prior auth and denial. Compounded liquid for kids who can't swallow tablets is NOT typically covered - cash-pay $40-150/month at compounding pharmacy. Calculation: tablet form at $9-36/month for generic; CalciFolinate brand $150+/month.

private_typical_coverage: Covered as B12 deficiency injection (J3420) only with documented deficiency; compounded methyl-B12 typically not covered
coverage_notes: Cyanocobalamin injection (J3420) is covered by Medicare and most commercial plans with documented B12 deficiency (E53.8) or pernicious anemia (D51.0); prior auth often required. Methylcobalamin specifically (the form Frye/Neubrander use for autism) is typically compounded and NOT covered by insurance - cash-pay $30-150/month at compounding pharmacy (Empower, Belmar, Hopewell). Some commercial plans cover preservative-free methylcobalamin under DME if billed with B12 deficiency dx. HSA/FSA covers cash-pay portion. Workaround: have the pediatrician document B12 deficiency before switching to methyl-B12; some plans then cover the methylated form. Subcutaneous home injection (parent-administered) is the Neubrander protocol.

private_typical_coverage: Off-label use of naltrexone at sub-therapeutic dose; compounded only; almost never insurance-covered
coverage_notes: Low-dose naltrexone (LDN) for autism, autoimmunity, chronic pain is off-label compounded use of naltrexone. The naltrexone-50mg-tablet (FDA-approved for alcohol/opioid use disorder) IS covered, but cannot be dosed at 1.5-4.5mg. Compounding pharmacies (Belmar, Skip's, ParkAve) make 1.5-4.5mg capsules; cash-pay $30-80/month. Insurance almost never covers compounded LDN regardless of indication. HSA/FSA cover the cash cost as a prescription. Coverage strategies: (1) Letter of medical necessity citing autoimmune dx (G93.3 myalgic encephalomyelitis, M35.9 systemic autoimmune); (2) Single-case agreement request with peer-to-peer review; (3) Mark-Allen/AllianceRx specialty pharmacy if MS/Crohn's dx documented.

private_typical_coverage: Generic sirolimus covered with TSC; off-label for autism without TSC typically denied
coverage_notes: Sirolimus (rapamycin) generic is FDA-approved for renal transplant rejection prophylaxis and SEGA in TSC patients. With TSC dx (Q85.1) plus autism phenotype, coverage is reliable; generic copay $0-50/month with prior auth. For autism without TSC (off-label mTOR-inhibition theory), coverage is rare - most plans deny as experimental for ASD. Brand Rapamune $400-800/month. Specialty tier on most formularies. Topical sirolimus (Hyftor) is FDA-approved for TSC facial angiofibromas, separate coverage. Anti-aging/longevity off-label use is never covered. HSA/FSA covers cash cost.

private_typical_coverage: FDA-approved for autism irritability ages 6-17; generic widely covered Tier 1-2
coverage_notes: Aripiprazole (Abilify) is FDA-approved for irritability associated with autistic disorder ages 6-17 (since 2009). Generic widely available, Tier 1-2 on most commercial formularies. Average $5-50/month for generic 5mg-15mg. Manufacturer coupon brings brand-name copay to $5 with commercial insurance. Medicaid covers nationwide. No prior auth required for FDA-labeled indication. The most reliably-covered atypical for autism (vs risperidone INT-0142 which is also FDA-approved). LAI formulation (Abilify Maintena/Asimtufii) carries different coverage requirements.

private_typical_coverage: Covered for primary immune deficiency reliably; for PANS/autism requires extensive documentation and frequent appeal
coverage_notes: IVIG bills as drug (J1568 Octagam, J1569 Gammagard, J1572 Flebogamma, J1599 unspecified) plus administration (96365 first hour infusion, 96366 each additional). 500mg unit billing. Per-infusion total $5,000-20,000 depending on weight and brand. Medicare reliably covers for primary immune deficiency (D80.x, D81.x, D83.x). PANS/PANDAS coverage (F95.x + G93.3 + D80.4 hypogammaglobulinemia documented) is highly plan-specific; ~30-50% approval rate without appeal. For autism alone (F84.0) without immune workup showing measurable deficiency, denial is near-universal. Successful coverage paths: (1) Document IgG subclass deficiency (D80.3) on workup; (2) PANS-specific NIMH consensus criteria documentation; (3) Specialty immunology referral establishing primary immunodeficiency. HSA/FSA covers patient-responsibility portion.

private_typical_coverage: OTC melatonin not covered; prescription Slenyto coverage varies
coverage_notes: Standard OTC melatonin (1-10mg) is NOT covered by insurance ever; cash-pay $5-30 per bottle, FSA/HSA eligible with LMN citing sleep disorder (G47.00). Prescription prolonged-release melatonin (Slenyto in EU, similar formulations available US through compounding) for autism-associated insomnia is FDA-approved EU/UK/Canada but NOT FDA-approved in US as of 2026 - therefore not formulary-covered by US payers. Compounded prolonged-release melatonin $30-80/month. Some pediatric specialty pharmacies (KidneyRx, etc) offer pediatric-dose melatonin gummies under HSA. Workaround: bill 90791/90834 psychotherapy with sleep-hygiene CBT for the behavioral component; the melatonin itself remains OOP.

private_typical_coverage: Off-label compounded nasal spray; failed phase 3 (SOARS-B 2021); no commercial coverage
coverage_notes: Intranasal oxytocin for autism is compounded by specialty pharmacies (Bayview, Empower, Harbor) at 40-100 IU/mL concentrations. Cash-pay $150-300/month. Not FDA-approved for any indication via intranasal route in the US; IV oxytocin (Pitocin) IS FDA-approved for labor induction but cannot be repurposed. SOARS-B (NEJM 2021) showed no benefit vs placebo, weakening any insurance appeal. No commercial plan covers compounded intranasal oxytocin for autism. HSA/FSA cover prescription cost. Coverage strategy: none reliable; counsel families this is OOP if pursued.

private_typical_coverage: Public sauna sessions or home equipment; HSA/FSA eligible with LMN
coverage_notes: Sauna therapy (infrared specifically) is not insurance-covered. Per-session at gym/spa $20-60; home infrared sauna $1500-5000. HSA/FSA eligible with Letter of Medical Necessity from licensed clinician documenting qualifying condition (chronic pain M79.x, autonomic dysregulation G90.x, mold-related illness, cardiovascular conditioning). Truemed and similar services facilitate LMN issuance and HSA card use. For autism, the LMN typically cites autonomic dysregulation (G90.8) or detoxification support. Best HSA/FSA play in functional medicine for ASD families with available pre-tax dollars - hardware lasts years.

private_typical_coverage: FDA-approved only for recurrent C. difficile; autism use is investigational; clinical trials only
coverage_notes: FMT bills under 44705 (preparation by physician) and G0455 (instillation) per Medicare. FDA-approved only for recurrent C. difficile infection that has failed standard antibiotic therapy (>=3 episodes). For autism, FMT is INVESTIGATIONAL - the only legal pathway is enrollment in an IRB-approved trial. ASU/Krajmalnik-Brown Microbiota Transfer Therapy trial cohorts have been studied since 2017. No commercial coverage for autism indication. Cash-pay clinics in Mexico/Australia $15,000-40,000 per multi-session protocol. NOT HSA/FSA eligible because not FDA-recognized for autism indication. Counsel families: clinical trial enrollment only.

private_typical_coverage: OTC supplement; HSA/FSA eligible with LMN
coverage_notes: Omega-3 (EPA + DHA) supplements - prescription Lovaza/Vascepa are covered for hypertriglyceridemia (E78.x); OTC fish oil for autism is never insurance-covered. Cash $15-80/month for therapeutic-dose EPA+DHA (1-2g/day for child). HSA/FSA eligible with LMN citing inflammation, neurodevelopmental disorder F84.0, or omega-3 deficiency. Truemed-style services issue LMNs at scale. The classic foundation supplement; cheapest reliable buy is high-dose Carlson, Nordic Naturals, or generic IFOS-certified EPA+DHA.

private_typical_coverage: OTC supplement; HSA/FSA eligible only with documented deficiency + LMN
coverage_notes: Vitamin D3 cholecalciferol OTC is never insurance-covered. HSA/FSA eligible ONLY with (a) documented deficiency (E55.9 - need 25-OH-D < 30 ng/mL on labs) AND (b) Letter of Medical Necessity from clinician. Cash $5-30/month for pediatric dose. Standalone vitamin without deficiency labs is treated by IRS as 'general health' - not HSA/FSA eligible. Workaround: order TEST-0043 first, document deficiency, then purchase supplement under LMN. Prescription-strength ergocalciferol (50,000 IU weekly) IS insurance-covered for severe deficiency with E55.9 - same molecule, just regulatory framing.

private_typical_coverage: OTC supplement; HSA/FSA eligible with LMN
coverage_notes: Magnesium glycinate OTC is never insurance-covered. HSA/FSA eligible with LMN citing magnesium deficiency (E61.2), sleep disorder (G47.00), constipation (K59.0), or muscle cramping (R25.2). Cash $8-30/month. Same IRS 'but-for' test as other supplements - the LMN must document the supplement is being used to treat a diagnosed condition, not for general wellness. RBC magnesium test (Quest) is the most defensible deficiency documentation.

private_typical_coverage: Gluten-free items reimbursable as incremental cost only; casein-free has no IRS recognition
coverage_notes: GFCF diet is not insurance-covered. HSA/FSA permits reimbursement of gluten-free items ONLY for the incremental cost above the standard equivalent (e.g., if GF bread is $7 and regular bread is $4, $3 is reimbursable) and ONLY with LMN documenting celiac disease (K90.0), wheat allergy (Z91.012), or non-celiac gluten sensitivity. Casein-free does not have a comparable IRS recognition unless milk allergy (Z91.011) documented. For autism without celiac, the LMN path is uncertain - the FCSN/TACA guidance is to document celiac/wheat-allergy testing first, then claim incremental costs. Annual receipt-tracking burden is high.

private_typical_coverage: OTC supplement; HSA/FSA eligible with LMN; clinical trial evidence is the supporting documentation
coverage_notes: Sulforaphane (from broccoli sprout extract or stabilized glucoraphanin/myrosinase) is not insurance-covered. Cash $30-60/month for therapeutic-dose products (Avmacol, Prostaphane). HSA/FSA eligible with LMN citing autism diagnosis (F84.0) and reference to Singh 2014 RCT supporting use. Some HSA administrators are conservative on this one because of the wellness/general-health framing; specifically request LMN reviewer at HSA administrator before submitting. Same logic applies to most other functional-medicine supplements (NAC, CoQ10, L-carnitine, P5P, methylfolate) - LMN required, eligibility depends on administrator interpretation.
